Lights flickering in the old tower, steady like a heartbeat
A sound from the ruins that carried for miles
Every animal in the area went silent at the same moment
The stream ran warm for half a day, then went back to normal
A patch of ground near the crossroads that won't hold frost
The fire in the common hall turned blue for a few minutes, no one touched it
Shadows moving the wrong direction in the late afternoon
A ring of dead grass appeared overnight in the clearing, perfectly round
The bells in the old shrine started ringing on their own
The wind changed direction four times in an hour, then stopped completely
Someone's compass spun in circles near the standing stones and wouldn't settle
The water in the rain barrel was ice cold in the middle of a warm spell
A crack opened in the foundation wall that wasn't there the day before
Birds flew in a tight circle over the village for most of the morning
The stars looked wrong that night, like something shifted overhead
Voices carrying from the hills, but nobody lives up there
Every candle on the street went out at the same time, no wind at all
A deep hum from underground that you could feel in your teeth
Moss growing in patterns on the old wall, almost like writing
The weather turned from clear to hail and back in the span of a meal
A tree at the crossroads split down the middle overnight, no storm
The sundial's shadow fell the wrong way for about an hour
Something left scorch marks on the standing stones, but there was no fire
The old well echoed back a voice that wasn't anyone's
Dust falling from the ceiling beams in the storehouse, rhythmic like footsteps above
A fog that rolled in from the hilltop instead of the valley
Fruit on the market tables rotted in minutes while everyone watched
The metalwork on the gate was warm to the touch at midnight
A flock of crows landed on every rooftop at once, sat for an hour, then left together
The echo in the canyon has been coming back wrong, like it's answering
The well water tasted like copper for three days, then went back to normal
A door in the old storehouse keeps opening on its own, even after they nailed it shut
The morning dew formed only on the west side of every surface, nowhere else
Someone heard singing from the sealed cellar beneath the council hall
Every mirror in the village fogged over at the same time, just before dawn
The chickens laid eggs with no yolks inside, three days running
A cold spot in the middle of the square that persists even at midday in summer
The old road marker started humming when you put your ear against it
Candle flames in the tavern all leaned the same direction, though every window was shut
The water in the fountain ran backwards for about ten minutes
An iron nail driven into the crossroads post turned to rust overnight
The sky turned a shade of green at sunset that no one had seen before
The flour in the bakery caked and hardened without moisture, as if baked by invisible heat
The flagstones in the courtyard were rearranged in the night: same stones, different pattern
A whispering sound from inside the walls of the old chapel, constant and low
The roosters crowed at midnight three nights in a row
Smoke from the chimneys drifted downward instead of rising, for the better part of an evening
The wooden beam over the main gate developed a crack in the shape of an eye
A section of the river froze solid in one spot while the rest flowed normally
The bread wouldn't rise anywhere in the settlement for a full day
A flash of light from the old ruins at exactly midnight, bright enough to cast shadows
Every piece of iron in the smithy was magnetized come morning: tools clinging to each other
The rainwater collected in the barrels had a faint glow to it, gone by sunrise
A line of ants marched in a perfect spiral around the well for hours and then dispersed
The grindstone in the mill started turning on its own in the middle of the night
Someone found footprints in the frost on the upper floor, but the stairs were undisturbed
The ink in the clerk's office dried to powder in every bottle at once
A section of wall in the old hall changed color overnight: from grey stone to pale white
The fish in the pond all surfaced at the same moment, floating still but alive
A low tone from beneath the standing stones, like a bell struck underground
The garden soil turned black in one patch and nothing will grow there now
The lock on the old vault seized shut and no key will turn it, though it worked fine yesterday
Every dog in the settlement howled at midday, all at once, then acted normal
The sundial cast two shadows for an hour, then went back to one
A puddle in the courtyard that never dries, even in heat and wind
The woodwork in the meeting hall sweated sap overnight, though the timber has been dry for years
Someone marked a tally on the old post: it was gone the next morning, and a new mark appeared three paces away
The torch in the watchtower burned without consuming the pitch for a full watch
A knocking from inside the sealed grain silo, three knocks repeated at intervals
The wool in the dyer's vats changed color on its own, from red to black
The weather vane on the council roof spun without stopping for an entire day
A pile of stones appeared on the doorstep of the abandoned house, neatly stacked
The creek water ran clear except for one stretch where it turned milky white
A bright point of light hovering over the field at night, stationary and unwavering
The old tree in the square dropped every leaf at once in the middle of the growing season
The cobblestones in the alley are warm in one spot, cold everywhere else
The settlement bell rang once at dawn, but the bellpull wasn't touched
A smell of flowers from the direction of the ruins, strong and sweet, in the dead of winter
The shadow of the watchtower stretched the wrong way at noon
Someone woke to find every piece of pottery in their house cracked, but still standing
The lamp oil burned green for an evening and no one could explain why
Water dripping upward from the floor of the old cellar, collecting on the ceiling beams
The wheat in one field grew a full hand's height overnight
A perfectly straight line of mushrooms appeared across the common, overnight
The air above the old well shimmers like heat haze, even in the cold
A stone thrown into the quarry pond took too long to splash: much too long
The evening meal tasted of ash for everyone who ate it, though the food looked fine
The shutters on the abandoned house open and close at regular intervals, no wind
Every piece of glass in the market hall developed a crack at the same moment
A column of warm air rising from a spot in the field where nothing is buried
The footprints in the dust of the sealed attic were fresh, but the ladder hadn't been moved
The river stones near the ford have been rearranged into a pattern that looks deliberate
Someone found their tools rearranged in the workshop, each one placed end to end in a line
The embers in the forge pit glowed for three days after the fire went out
A sound like a massive door closing, from somewhere underground, felt more than heard
The spiderweb in the barn doorway was woven in a pattern too regular to be natural
The watering trough froze solid in the middle of the afternoon, then thawed an hour later
Every flame in the settlement flickered at the same instant, then steadied
A vibration in the floor of the old chapel that comes and goes with no pattern anyone can find
The path through the woods is shorter than it was last month, and nobody can explain why
An echo that arrives before the sound: someone claps, and the echo comes first
The mortar between the stones of the old wall is weeping: damp and dark, but it hasn't rained
The barley in the storehouse sprouted overnight inside the sealed sacks
The old bronze sundial turned green overnight, the kind of patina that takes years
A smell of burning from the abandoned mine entrance, but no smoke and no heat
The settlement dogs all dug holes in the same spot, one after another, then filled them back in
A haze over the lake that moves against the wind and reforms when it disperses
The cistern water level drops every night and refills by morning, but no one's drawing from it
The wooden sign outside the inn started bleeding sap in a line that looks like a letter
The stone floor of the common hall is colder than the ground outside, even with the fire going
A shadow passed over the settlement at noon, but the sky was perfectly clear
The grain in the field nearest the ruins won't thresh: the husks cling to the kernel as if fused
The trees along the road have all bent slightly in the same direction, though no storm has come
The candles in the shrine melted into shapes that looked like figures
A dry patch in the stream bed that the water flows around, not over
The old foundation hummed so loud at night that it woke the family in the nearest house
Dust motes in the meeting hall sunbeam hung perfectly still, not drifting at all
The butter churned in half the time, and it tasted wrong: bitter and metallic
A trail of salt-white residue appeared on the ground in a line from the well to the gate
The forge fire refused to light for a day, no matter what fuel or tinder was used
A single flower bloomed in the dead garden behind the abandoned house, out of season
The wind carried a word on it, just once, clear as a voice: nobody agrees on what it said
The clay tiles on the oldest roof cracked in a pattern that radiates from the center
The settlement sundial was accurate for years: now it's off by a full hour, consistently
The water in every bucket left out overnight turned slightly pink by morning
A tree stump in the clearing is warm to the touch all the time, summer or winter
The night sky had a faint reddish glow to the east, but there's no settlement in that direction
A stone in the old wall shifted forward by a finger's width overnight, perfectly level
The tallow candles in the storehouse all bent toward the east side, as if pulled
The wooden stakes marking the new field plot sank into the ground on their own, straight down
A single lightning bolt struck the standing stone at midnight, in a clear sky
The draft in the old tunnel reversed direction for an hour and carried a smell of earth and copper
The path markers on the hill trail all faced a different direction come morning
Dew collected in the shape of handprints on the doorstep of the council hall
The old well chain links have stretched: they were tight a month ago, now they're slack
The air over the crossroads tasted metallic for a whole afternoon
A sound like hammering from the sealed mine, rhythmic and steady, going on for hours
The grain sacks in the corner of the storehouse were too heavy to lift, though nothing was added
The carved face on the keystone over the gate changed expression: mouth closed before, now open
The smoke from the settlement fires formed a ring overhead and held its shape for an hour
A patch of ice formed on the road in the middle of a warm week, round and perfectly smooth
The latch on the shrine door works itself open every night, no matter how firmly it's set
Every pot of water left to boil whistled at the same pitch at the same time

A ring of discolored earth appeared around the old well, expanding by a hand's width each day
The tavern clock stopped at the same time three days running, then worked fine the rest of the day
The horses all faced the same direction in their stalls at dawn, standing perfectly still
A taste of iron in the air near the abandoned forge, though the fire has been cold for years
The shadows under the meeting hall table moved when no one else in the room did
The creek stones downstream from the ruins have turned black, though the water seems clean
The knots in the floorboards of the old house have begun to weep a dark, sticky fluid
A sound like glass breaking from underground, repeated twice, then silence for days
The birds that nest in the eaves all moved to the far side of the building on the same morning
The bread baked near the standing stones came out with a bitter crust, though the recipe was the same
A circle of warmth on the ground near the old marker, noticeable even through boot leather
The rope in the belltower frayed in a pattern: every third strand cut, the rest intact
The washwater turned red when poured on the ground behind the chapel, clear everywhere else
A draft from the sealed basement that smells like wildflowers and decay in equal measure

The path around the old cairn takes longer to walk than it should: people keep timing it
The dew on the morning grass formed droplets that were perfectly uniform in size

The reflection in the pond showed clouds that weren't in the actual sky
The temperature dropped so fast in the square that breath fogged, then it was warm again within minutes

A sound from the root cellar like someone dragging a chair across the floor, but the cellar is empty
The candlemaker's wicks all curled in the same direction while cooling

The rain fell in a line: dry on one side of the road, pouring on the other
The charcoal in a dead fire pit rearranged itself into a circle overnight

A sound like a deep intake of breath from the mouth of the old cave, once at sunset

Every leather strap in the tack room dried and cracked on the same night
The flame on the altar candle burned steadily for a week without the candle shrinking
A patch of darkness in the corner of the storehouse that lantern light won't reach
The old road surface rippled like water for a moment, then went solid again
Frost formed on the inside of a window that faces the standing stones, only on that window

A patch of air above the old grave site that refracts light, like looking through water

A sound like a horn blast from deep underground that only some people could hear

The grain stored in the old tower germinated inside the sacks, stalks pushing through the burlap

The rain fell warm near the crossroads: not tepid, warm, like bathwater

The echo in the well shaft repeated words that hadn't been spoken into it

The night sky went completely dark for a count of ten: no moon, no stars, nothing, then everything returned
The pile of firewood near the hall restacked itself: the pieces that were on top are now on the bottom
A puddle near the old marker stone showed a reflection of clear sky when the actual sky was overcast
The milk from every cow in the settlement curdled at the same hour, and the cheese made from it tasted of copper

A smell of woodsmoke lingered in the abandoned house for a day, though no fire has been lit there in months
